I think the reason this is happening is because of a custom Strawberry Perl build, that inlcudes a slightly altered MinGW (it packages GCC 6.3.0 using SEH exceptions). I had to uninstall that and install normal MinGW in order to make gdbgui work properly. Unfortunately then the exact program I want to debug doesn't compile anymore. So I had to go back to the other MinGW and now get these errors again.

Do you have any idea whats going on and how I could maybe even fix it by myself? Doesn't gdbgui work on gcc 6.3 and SEH exceptions? 
A start would be to handle that `e is undefined` error, but I don't know if there will be other errors down the road because of the MinGW mismatch.

I can't speak as to why you are getting altered responses from gdb itself. Maybe there is some custom handling or preprocessing of gdb responses. I do know why the screen goes white though. It's an unfortunate result of the way javascript exceptions are handled in the app. I could try to "try/catch" everywhere instead of just going totally white, but I don't because this is the first time I've heard of this happening, and I don't really expect it to ever happen.

If you want to try to debug/fix this yourself, I recommend following [these instructions](https://github.com/cs01/gdbgui/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md). In particular, look at the file Threads.jsx.

I also just pushed a branch to `issue-169` which might fix your issue. You can clone and run it and see how it works.

#!/usr/bin/env python3

import threading


def set_interval(fn, sec):
    def wrapper_fn():
        fn()
        set_interval(fn, sec)

    t = threading.Timer(sec, wrapper_fn)
    t.start()
    return t

I just saw that ethernet.py does a similar thing on line 107 for MPLS.
 self.type = ETH_TYPE_IP

You can't just overwrite the outside ethertype with the inner ethertype. It clobbers that data and then it's impossible write the original packet back to disk.

I'll need to investigate everywhere a statement like self.type = ETH_TYPE_X is made in ethernet.py. There's another one in the IPX decapsulation, but I don't really know enough about IPX to understand the implications.

If you generate base-alignment with `-c` or `-a`, minimap2 may align through the gap with another "long-join" heuristic that allows longer gaps. You can disable this heuristic with `--no-long-join`.
I have tested this feature and it works perfectly for my test cases.

@Kanthavel-Securibox :

Semantically, dicts in PDF files are not the same as Python dicts, and pdfrw's PdfDict is designed to match a PDF dict's behavior as closely as possible.  One feature of PDF dicts is that there is no difference between a missing key and a key that has a null value.  pdfrw supports this by never raising a `KeyError` exception -- it returns `None` (pdfrw's version of a PDF null) instead.  So, instead of:

`if y.pages and y.pages[0] and '/MediaBox' in y.pages[0] and len(y.pages[0].get('/MediaBox')) > 3:`

You can write:

`if y.pages and y.pages[0] and len(y.pages[0].MediaBox or ()) > 3:`

Hi Matti, 
this is definitely a problem related to a misbehaving server. The LDAP protocol specify that only one LDAP response must be returned for a Bind request even when using SASL. What error do you get from ldap3? It should raise an LDAPSocketReceiveError exception with the "invalid messageID received" message. 
If this is the case and you're using the SYNC strategy you can check  the commented code starting at line 199 of ldap3.strategy.sync.py that handle this specific case. Keep in mind that you will get this exception only when you send the next LDAP request.

I don't know about ldapsearch but I think it works the same. It don't intentionally "merges" the two responses but it ignore the second one.

If you want to monkeypatch it you should catch the exception and manually adjust the ``server._message_counter`` variable to the next valid message id. Also you must set the ``connection.bound`` variable to ``True``. I think this should be enough, but I think that you should fix the server that probably has a SASL method badly configured.

I believe there is not a straightforward fix for this issue.

I did put together a patch (attached) which simply prepends the username to the servicename in the WinVault target. Although this patch addresses the issue (and causes the failing test to pass), it will have the unfortunate consequence of invalidating all passwords stored by previous versions of keyring. Furthermore, this causes the username to always be stored in the target, even though the username is also specified in the password record, so that field ends up being stored redundantly.

It seems the WinVault and python-keyring paradigms are somewhat in conflict here: WinVault expects the target to specify only one password for a given service (per Windows User context), but python-keyring expects to be able to store multiple passwords per service (one additional per username).

I'm considering an alternative approach which would maintain backward compatibility, specified thus:

* Passwords will be stored under their simple service name first, and moved to a compound service name, "username@servicename" if there is a collision caused by a subsequent set operation.
* When a password is set, it is always stored under its simple service name. If there is already a service defined under than name but with a different username, that credential is re-saved under its compound service name. Also, any compound service name corresponding to the new credential should be removed (if present).
* When a password is read for a given service, it will be queried first under a simple service name, then under a compound service name.
* When a password is deleted, the delete must be attempted under both the simple and compound service name.

I realize this seems unnecessarily convoluted, but it serves two major benefits:

# It's backward compatible, so existing users of keyring won't feel any pain when adopting this change. 
# In the most common use-case, where a single Windows user has exactly one password for a given service, the username isn't stored redundantly in the target. The user has his sole password for that service, and the associated username is stored only in the metadata fields for that credential and not in the service identifier.

I'll mull this over for a while, but I'd like to hear feedback from the community as well if there are any opinions on this issue.

Added a new commit

> Just return an empty string in cwd() method of _psaix.py instead of None.

I noticed that the test for `cwd` already checks whether the return value is `None` (which can also happen on BSD and Solaris), but the test checks if it's `None` after making sure it's a string, which means it's a bug in the test. I fixed the test instead. I also fixed some other failures in `test_fetch_all`

> test_setup_script

I realized why it failed. It's because I used `python setup.py test` instead of `make test` to run the unit tests. Running the former makes argv ok for setup.setup() so it doesn't raise SystemExit with usage. I'll use `make test` from now on.

> OK, you said create_zombie_proc() doesn't work on AIX. Have you checked whether ps is able to detect it though? I just want to be sure it's not status()

OK, so it turns out you *can* create zombie processes and there was indeed a bug in `status`. It's not simple to determine actual process state from /proc, but I think I added the code required.

> I would just change the test and do a comparison of the first 30 chars if you're on AIX

The problem isn't that we cut to 30 characters (according to the struct in `/proc` the max should be 80), but that there is a single argument `import time; time.sleep(60)` which is too long so it's somehow truncated from `psinfo`. If I add another argument to argv after this argument, it will show in psinfo while "skipping" the long argument.

> It means pid_exists() is broken

The PIDs in question do exist, in `ps` and in `/proc`. They have `/proc/<pid>/psinfo`, and even `psutil.Process(pid)` works on them. Yet pid_exists returns False, because the "send a signal" check, even when trying with Python's `os.kill`, raises `OSError: no such process`. I noticed these processes are all named `wait` and it appears that there is 1 per logical CPU. It's an AIX thing...

> The test may also not pass but we should never have [''] returned.

I traced this back to an issue with our environment, again. `create_exe` used to compile C code for the test executable but now it's using `sys.executable` instead (also, there's a bug in that function where the default code is never used). Apparently `sys.executable` is not copy-able on our environment (again the `execve python2.7.bin` trick) so when running it it immediately exits, and that's why we get the empty cmdline. When changing `create_exe` (not committed, of course), those tests pass except `test_prog_w_funky_name` which, again, doesn't pass on Solaris either.

> What about memory leak tests?

Those tests pass now after a few small changes.

The usual way to do what you are doing is what I described as option 1 above.  Use a nearby redshift bin for the lenses and a more distant redshift bin for the sources.  Then correlate everything in the foreground with everything in the background, and you'll have zG < zg for all pairs.

In particular, this has a well-defined theoretical prediction for the signal, which you need in order to do anything useful with the measurement.  It would be much harder to get an accurate prediction for the measurement you are proposing where you take all sources in the background of any lens, especially considering the sample variance in the distribution of lenses and the uncertainties in the photo-zs.

I'm not opposed to having a generic configurable adapter.  I'm definitely not opposed to having more developer contribution. :-) But there are a couple hurdles to get over first.

The first is proving that it's real-world useful with FFF.  For that, I'd like to see working configurations for either some of the requested sites, some existing supported sites, or both.  If it can't be configured to support say 3-4 known sites, I have to question how many other sites it would be useful for.  Also keep in mind that because FFF runs as a Google web service, python pip CLI _and_ a Calibre plugin, including non-standard libraries can be problematic--we already have to bundle several libraries in with FFF.

The second is that the existing FFF architecture is pretty firmly site==adapter==adapter source file.  We have more than one site using the same code in places, but we generally do it by class inheritance.  For proof-of-concept and initial testing, that's fine: add a new `base_whatever_adapter.py` file, add `adapter_sitename.py` files for each configured site, add the parser control configurations to `defaults.ini`.  If a generic/configable adapter proves to be workable, and sufficiently easy for users to do, I'll need to then extend the architecture to allow sites to be added without `adapter_sitename.py` files.

If all that sounds reasonable, I suggest that we open a new issue for futher discussion.

Okay, to summarize: 
- The site webnovel.com is now putting an ad-wall in front of the full text for the last two chapters of stories.
- There's nothing in the TOC (other than 'the last two') to indicate ad-walled chapters.
- @cryzed reports that getting the chapter text passed the ad is straight forward.
- @cryzed further reports that there's a REST API for retrieving chapter content (that includes a 'require ad' flag).

I wonder if there's a REST API for the TOC?  And if it would include that flag for ad-walled chapters?

Our options seem to be:
1. Bypass the ad-wall and download all chapters in the TOC as normal, either by HTML scrape or JSON/REST API.
1. Discard the 'bonus chapters' (ie, ad-walled chapters) from the TOC, making them invisible to FFF.  Either by removing the last two (configurably?) or from reading the flags from a REST call, if available.  (Fetching all chapter URLs to get the flags is not a good solution.)

I agree with @cryzed's concern that ad-walling may appear on other chapters without warning--removing the last X chapters from TOC is one thing, removing every other chapter is another.  So that argues against option 2.

My instinct is to go for option 1.  Or remove support for the site if it becomes an on-going arms race.

Although the reset_password_token is:

- user specific,
- only valid for two days, and
- encrypted and signed

@3noch does have a point that a hacker with access to the DB would be able to reset the passwords for users that have started (but not completed) a password reset in the past two days.

It turns out that the reset_password_token DB field was a remnant of a v0.1 implementation and the workaround is quite simple: Remove the reset_password_token from the User (or UserAuth) table. Flask-User v0.5 and up will work without this DB field.

I will update all the docs and example apps to reflect this increased security approach.

Thank you @3noch for taking the time to report this. It's the open-source concept with community members like you that make the internet a safer place.

Fixed in v0.5+ by removing the reset_password_token from the User (or UserAuth) table.

i'm not sure it's a good idea to use freetype's own version number. It's true we always want to bump the wrapper's version whenever the wrapped library (or librarie*s*) is updated, like in this case. But we may well have bugfixes or new features added to the wrapper which don't entails updating the embedded libs' versions. You could in theory use [post releases](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#post-releases) for that (e.g. `2.9.0.post1`, `2.9.0.post2`, etc.) but you would then only have a single incremental number to distinguish among different post-releases, so no more semver.org distinction between major.minor.patch.
I'd recommend keeping the two version strings (the python wrapper and the bundled freetype and harfbuzz libraries) separate from each other.

Currently it looks like the `--server` flag is used to specify a certain server *id* from the current speedtest servers list. You might be able to add arbitrary servers by modifying where the program looks for server lists or to override this flag or add another flag to do so. However, it seems like the server you specify must conform to a certain protocol or have certain endpoints to be able to correctly upload to and download files from it to test your connection speed. So putting an arbitrary third-party server won't work because it doesn't have those endpoints and work in the same manner. Also, it's may not be clear where the third-party server is located, and I think one of the values of speedtest is to reduce geographical distance between you and the server to take that away as a variable.

I can't say that this is not on the roadmap, but it seems that unless you had an open source speedtest server that it would be difficult to point to a third-party server. Hope this helps!

Hi,

> When formatting a row, I lose the column formatting.

In Excel a cell format overrides a row format which overrides a column format. The only way to combine a row and column format would be to set a cell format that is a combination of the two (this is what Excel does invisibly in the background). However, that isn't currently possible with the Pandas - XlsxWriter interface.

> The below code works for a Pandas Dataframe, but it is hard coded and does not consider the dynamically generated rows, as the rows can change based on what the dataframe pulls.

There are several [Cell Utility Functions](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/working_with_cell_notation.html#cell-utility-functions) available in XlsxWriter that can be used to create cell ranges. The `conditional_format()`

> While formulas such as the below work for isolating the conditional formatting to one cell, I've not found a good solution for row based conditional formatting.

This is really an Excel issue, right? If you can define a conditional format in Excel that works you can transfer it to XlsxWriter.

Correct, that is one of the new incompatible changes for the upcoming major version v2.x. 

Previously all records and shapes were stored in memory as attributes on the Writer class, which limits how large shapefiles it is possible to write. This limitation is removed in the upcoming major version, so that the writer now writes each record/shape immediately to a temporary file. It's a similar streaming approach as the `csv` module. The "Working with Large Shapefiles" section of the Readme explains the new behavior. 

This will mean that you can't inspect which records or shapes you have written after the fact, but to check how many entries you have written you can instead call `len(ws)`. I will likely add a large note at the top of the v2 Readme to explain the main changes such as this.

I ran into the same problem. Twitch accepts the header 'Authorization: OAuth <access_token>' and not the OAuth2 'Authorization: Bearer ...' header. You can find the fix for this in the  `pre_request` example for Weibo in [Fixing Non-Standard OAuth](http://flask-oauthlib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/client.html#fix-non-standard-oauth)
```
def change_twitch_header(uri, headers, body):
    auth = headers.get('Authorization')
    if auth:
        auth = auth.replace('Bearer', 'OAuth')
        headers['Authorization'] = auth
    return uri, headers, body
```
And then make a pre_request before twitch.get()
    twitch.pre_request = change_twitch_header

[Here's the relevant function in oauthlib](https://github.com/idan/oauthlib/blob/60b5a353d5d63de0430fe8b6fab253555510c1d3/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/clients/base.py#L145). Hope that helps.

Use a [virtual environment](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/dev/virtualenvs/). mwparserfromhell is no different from other python software in this case.

Your variable `w` is not a string (`str`), but a `Wikicode` object. Its `replace` method (documented [here](https://mwparserfromhell.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/mwparserfromhell.html#mwparserfromhell.wikicode.Wikicode.replace)) works differently than `str.replace`, the `ValueError` exception is raised regardless of the parameter type, which can be a `Node`, `Wikicode` or `str`. Given `str` is first parsed into another `Wikicode` object, hence the exception for consistency.

If you want to do `str`-like replacement and get a `str` instead of `Wikicode`, do `str(w).replace("doesntexist", "")`.

It seems that in IE, the 'input' event is triggered on input elements that have a placeholder attribute set.
See [this](https://connect.microsoft.com/IE/feedback/details/810538/) or [this](http://www.carsonshold.com/2013/12/js-bug-ie-10-and-11-oninput-event-with-placeholder-set/).

A solution would be to change stConfig.search.inputEvent to 'keypress' instead of 'input'(the default), or simply remove the placeholder attribute from the input element.

That is very odd. Django serializes timestamps as ISO 8601 date strings,
which includes microsecond values.

The bug is almost certainly either in Django's serialization framework, or
your own code. Something, somewhere, discarded those microseconds between
saving revisions. django-reversion just trusts Django's serializers to do
the right thing.

I think we could add a check to ensure the stream is still alive. Effectively, this would make errors be swallowed silently. I don't like that kind of approach, but I guess in this case it would OK to do like that.

The thing is, since the standard logging module does not synchronize `addHandler`/`removeHandler` with actual access to a handler, a worker thread can indeed call an already detached and closed handler. 

There is an inevitable race condition anyway:
 - worker thread sees the handler attached the logger (`for handler in logger.handlers: ...`)
 - main thread removes the handler and closes it (`logger.handlers.remove(handler); handler.close()`)
 - worker thread calls `handler.handle(...)`, which throws the error in question

Hence, there is no fundamental difference, whether the handler is invoked due to a race condition just after it has been closed, or it is not invoked at all because it has been removed already. Let me show that on your example:

```py
with pytest_catchlog.common.catching_logs(handler_setup):
    ...
    thread.start()
    # <- if a worker thread logs now, the `handler_setup` captures the record
# <- if somewhere at catching_logs(...).__exit__, you get the "I/O operation on closed file" error

# <- if a worker logs in between these blocks, a record is lost anyway

with pytest_catchlog.common.catching_logs(handler_body):
    # <- a record is captured by the `handler_body`
    ...
```

Does is make sense for your case?

@The-Compiler answering your comment from [here](https://github.com/eisensheng/pytest-catchlog/pull/7#issuecomment-144712274):
> Note subclassing `list` isn't a good idea as subclassing a builtin comes with many fun surprises when C code uses `list` instead of the subclass internally. Something like this would be better I think (untested):
> ...

I decided to inherit the builtin types directly nonetheless, because we actually *do* want the object to be treated as `list`/`str` instead of the subclass. After all, we only add the `__call__` slot that is not used internally anyway.

Long story short, this is due to the use of `packaging`.  The current Homebrew/Homebrew-cask's version (1.5.12-9-g49927ed) parses as a [LegacyVersion](https://packaging.pypa.io/en/latest/version/#packaging.version.LegacyVersion).  The version we check against (1.1.12) parses as a [Version](https://packaging.pypa.io/en/latest/version/#packaging.version.Version).  Per the documentation, a LegacyVersion is *always* considered less than a Version.  The requirement for Homebrew ('>=1.0.*') also parses as a LegacyVersion which is why it hasn't failed.  Changing the requirement to `>=1.1.12-*` seems to resolve the issue. Opening a PR for that.

I have pyinstaller working with flask-socketio and eventlet. There are **two** hidden imports that you need to reference:

engineio.async_eventlet
eventlet.support.dns

Yes, I understand that is how you want to do it. But as you said, it is not safe and not clean. That is why we don't do it that way in Sanic.

While Sanic is 'Flask-like' in a lot of ways, this is one of the cases where it is quite different. And mimicking the Flask-way and trying to use Sanic as if it was Flask is not a good idea.

The SPF 'shared context' is the closest thing we have a flask-like app_context. That is, it is a context object (a special dict) provided by SPF that all plugins and the app itself can access.

The Contextualize plugin can allow you to use the shared context in your app if you aren't writing your own plugin.

The contextualize example.py file mentioned above does show how to do it, but it may be not very clear.

In the next few days I'll create a specific sqalchemy example that shows exactly how to do what you want, using the Contextualize plugin.

The code snippet below builds a pruned, quantized 5-gram language model that is significantly better than the ["quick-fix" language model](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/pull/1487).

The corpus used is described in section `4. Language Models` of the [original LibriSpeech paper](https://www.danielpovey.com/files/2015_icassp_librispeech.pdf).

With little to no optimisation or hyper-parameter tuning we get a `dev-clean` WER of ~6.4 on a version of our internal implementation of DS1. You can adjust the order, pruning level, and quantization level to suit your needs :-) (e.g. a 3-gram 8-bit binary trie is <1GB and has a `dev-clean` WER of ~6.5) 

Note that this code was written in a Jupyter notebook and uses the `lmplz` and `build_binary` commands from the [kenlm library](https://github.com/kpu/kenlm). The resulting language model is ~1.7GB. I've sent this to @kdavis-mozilla via IRC already.

```python
import gzip
import io
import os

from urllib import request

# Grab corpus.
url = 'http://www.openslr.org/resources/11/librispeech-lm-norm.txt.gz'
data_upper = '/tmp/upper.txt.gz'
request.urlretrieve(url, data_upper)

# Convert to lowercase and cleanup.
data_lower = '/tmp/lower.txt'
with open(data_lower, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as lower:
    with io.TextIOWrapper(io.BufferedReader(gzip.open(data_upper)), encoding='utf8') as upper:
        for line in upper:
            lower.write(line.lower())
os.remove(data_upper)

# Build pruned LM.
lm_path = '/tmp/lm.arpa'
!lmplz --order 5 \
       --temp_prefix /tmp/ \
       --memory 50% \
       --text {data_lower} \
       --arpa {lm_path} \
       --prune 0 0 0 1

# Quantize and produce trie binary.
binary_path = '/tmp/lm.binary'
!build_binary -a 255 \
              -q 8 \
              trie \
              {lm_path} \
              {binary_path} 
os.remove(lm_path)
```

I'm not sure this is the same issue but I think it is, so here's another (possible) use case that doesn't seem to be supported with current syntax I can find documented:

The cleanest way I have found to use system-installed pyqt(5) in a venv is to install vext.pyqt5, which basically installs shims to use the system copy of pyqt5 in the virtual environment. For some reason this package does not play nice when installed from wheel - and I agree this is probably a bug in the package that should be addresssed - so in a classic venv workflow you would install with:

`pip install --no-binary vext.pyqt5 vext.pyqt5`

I have confirmed that using this variant of the install command works (on previous pip versions it would apparently have been `--no-use-wheel`). Unfortunately I cannot find any way to specify this package in my Pipfile and have it get installed when I spin up an environment. I've tried things along the line of this: 
`"vext.pyqt5" = "*"`
and
`"vext.pyqt5" = {version = "*", install-option = "--no-binary vext.pyqt5"}`

I have not had any success with this in my Pipfile when I try to initially create an environment using `pipenv install`. I get a warning about the failed package installation, a notice that it's retrying, this appears to succeed (the command finishes w/o error), and the package is listed as installed but non-functional. 

Interestingly: if I leave this out of my Pipfile initially, and then after installing the rest of the environment I use `pipenv install vext.pyqt5` to install the package, that command reports a (test?) error but I get an entry added to my Pipfile *and* a functional package. Actually, I don't much like idea of adding some sort of hidden arguments to each query method.

This way it would be hard to write new method that would receive `**kwargs` and do something with it as with `dict` (like `command()` or `distinct()` create DB query from `kwargs`) without hidden knowledge that some sort of underscored arguments may appear in `kwargs` and needs to be removed aforehead. 

Or if someone will introduce new query method and will fail to follow guideline of receiving `**kwargs` and mandatory passing it to all subcalls, the deadline feature will be broken. And this guideline isn't obvious from the code without studying internals of `@timeout`. This looks like a sign of fragile design that is easy to break.

You changes for supporting deadline is pretty broad already. Isn't it better to remove `inlineCallbacks` from query methods and get benefit from Twisted's builtin cancellation mechanism? This would be a massive change too, but it will allow us to keep deadline feature separate from internals of _each_ query method.

@ikondov Thanks!

My only hesitation in merging this is to try to avoid confusion / problems for users on things like distinction between PyTask and PythonFunctionTask or ScriptTask and CommandLineTask. There should ideally be just one of each and that one would encompass all use cases.

For PyTask and PythonFunctionTask, it seems there should be a way to modify PyTask to do what you want without too much effort. Perhaps just allowing inputs to be an alternate for args and having an option to return things in a certain format? I think it will be a much better situation to have one task rather than two different ones. Note that PyTask doesn't require you to return a FWAction, and there is already a "stored_data_varname" that helps you return a custom FWAction. You could another custom parameter that helps PyTask return the type of FWAction you want for data processing.

For ScriptTask vs CommandLineTask, this one looks a bit harder to merge one into the other. But, if you had thoughts on how to do that, it would be better to have just one.

I interpreted it as an example of a [Linear Assignment Problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assignment_problem), which is a well studied class of problems, and the most common solution is in the form of essentially a global optimisation algorithm (e.g. the Hungarian algorithm, or Jonker-Volgenant which I referenced above - `lapjv`).

For completeness, the following code demonstrates the behaviour in ``ndi.map_coordinates``.  Comparing to my previous comment above, only mode 'wrap' is different.

```python
import scipy.ndimage as ndi
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

img = np.zeros((9, 9))
img[:8, :8] += 1
img[:4, :4] += 1
img[:2, :2] += 1
img[:1, :1] += 2
img[8, 8] = 4

rows, cols = img.shape[0], img.shape[1]
map_rows, map_cols = np.mgrid[-rows:2*rows, -cols:2*cols]
coord_map = np.array([map_rows, map_cols])
order=1, cval=0
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(15, 5))
for n, mode in enumerate(['constant', 'nearest', 'wrap', 'reflect', 'mirror']):
    out = ndi.map_coordinates(img, coord_map, order=order,
                              mode=mode, cval=cval)
    axes[n].imshow(out, cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
    axes[n].plot([8.5, 8.5], [8.5, 17.5], 'y--', linewidth=0.5)
    axes[n].plot([17.5, 17.5], [8.5, 17.5], 'y--', linewidth=0.5)
    axes[n].plot([8.5, 17.5], [8.5, 8.5], 'y--', linewidth=0.5)
    axes[n].plot([8.5, 17.5], [17.5, 17.5], 'y--', linewidth=0.5)
    axes[n].set_axis_off()
    axes[n].set_aspect('equal')
    axes[n].set_title(mode)
plt.tight_layout()
```

FYI, I believe it's spelled "vihara" (the name for a Buddhist monastery).

I don't think we need to treat this topic as an all-or-nothing affair though. I don't think it's the same as genders. Icons for houses of worship (church/mosque/temple/monestery etc) should be treated on a case-by-case basis, based on demand, just like any other icon request. I would view it similar to requests for sports, or famous product logos (Facebook/WhatsApp/Google etc) - eg. if lots of people request an icon for a tennis ball, you would consider adding it, but you wouldn't decide that you have to add every other sport on the planet at the same time, in fear of offending croquet players. :)

I honestly don't think that people will be offended if you add one icon for one house of worship, based on how popular it is, while leaving other ones until there is more demand for them or you have more time. Otherwise it's not very fair when there is massive demand for one particular icon, which many webmasters are desperately wanting to use for their client projects - they shouldn't have to wait until more demand is added for less common icons. It should be a simple case of supply and demand.

Worth noting that the fa-church icon request page #2088 currently has 32 "+1" comments on it, making it a very popular request, while the fa-mosque page #5146 has 4 "+1" comments. I can't find a request for synagogues or temples yet.

You could at least just create icons for a church and a mosque, as there is at least demand for those two, but I don't see the point in spending time to create icons for other things that (hardly) nobody needs.

Let's return to to original topic.
Sure, we can push replay_location for every node into SIS. It's not a big problem and very useful.
But master cant generate a lot of wal segments. For example some of our databases generating ~10GB/hour (or ~4MB/sec). Keeping in mind that each node pushes data into SIS at a different time do you really want to make some predictions in this case? And how good they would be?

Actually I  did some performance testing of our API module. On my it can handle ~250 requests/sec. Here I am talking about real http/1.0 requests, i.e. without keep-alive.
So connecting to all nodes in parallel and fetching status in best case will take less then 0.1 sec. In worst case some requests will hit connection timeout.

And one more important question. What if somebody just killed patroni/governor with "-9". In this case postgres will stay alive. Should we consider this scenario when checking that former "master/leader" (according to SIS) is not reachable? I mean not patroni/governor, but the real postgres.

GLPK is not really suited for anything but toy-sized MILPs (e.g. it solved 0 out of 87 MIPLIB 2010 benchmark problems).  The MILPs arising from medium sized GEMs are too big for GLPK to handle (too many binary variables) and frequently badly-scaled (causing numerical difficulties). The non-commerical SCIP solver might be an alternative but I haven't tried it out.

My suggestion is that unless Gurobi/CPLEX are available, the MILP-based tests should not be executed.

Sidenote: The memote report(s) should include information on the solver used during testing and the version of cobraPy.

Thank you Andy, that was the key to the problem.

Indeed the versions of the requests package on the Ubuntu and Fedora machines (v.2.2.1 and v1.2.3) did not match the one bundled into the PEX file. After an upgrade with pip the SSL exception was raised consistently by the PEX application and the python script.

I'll explain the final resolution if case someone runs into similar trouble and comes to this issue. The problem was not the version of the requests package but the fact that the package was installed from the distro repository (yum/apt). Those packages have the [certs.py](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/certs.py) customized to point to the respective location of the CA certificate bundle. However the pants tool pulled the "pure" version of the package which does not know the correct location of the bundle. It provides [its own bundle](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/cacert.pem), hence it was unable to find the SSL certificates that I had deployed.

That was a good lesson to learn. Thanks again.

Hi @scizers ,

I've managed to make it work. However, you must use the MathML to Office MathML stylesheet in order to transform the MathML data into Office-compatible MathML.

The stylesheet is installed along with Microsoft Office and you can find it in:
`C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Office\Office14\MML2OMML.XSL`.

```
#!/bin/env python
# coding: utf-8

from lxml import etree

# Convert MathML (MML) into Office MathML (OMML) using a XSLT stylesheet
tree = etree.fromstring(mathml_string)
xslt = etree.parse(mml2omml_stylesheet_path)

transform = etree.XSLT(xslt)
new_dom = transform(tree)

paragraph = doc.add_paragraph()
paragraph._element.append(new_dom.getroot())
```

You may also need to remove any namespace from the `<math>` tag before transforming it into Office MathML. Let me know if it works for you too.

> I'm wondering if option 1 is too restrictive. That way, a plugin cannot access its own models when it's being enabled. On the other hand, things like migrations may not have been fired.

Following up on this: Since one may run `enable->disable->enable`, with each operation starting and exiting Kolibri, I believe that plugins should not make lots of assumptions about initial states while running `enable`. This is just a caution.

Anyways, because commands such as `collectstatic` and `migrate` have to be run, we also have to run `django.setup()` anyways.

Here's a rough vision of what enabling a plugin could look like:

``` 
$ kolibri plugin enable kolibri.plugins.learn

Enabling plugin: kolibri.plugins.learn

Verifying dependencies... OK!
Collecting static assets (JS/CSS/images)........ OK!
Running database migrations............ OK!

Plugin enabled. Please start/restart Kolibri.
```

Yes, unfortunately that will be necessary.

It's been tough, thanks!

Two options:

1) Running `django.setup()` before enabling/disabling a plugin. That way, we just *know* that the django stack is reading when loading `kolibri_plugin` which requires less carefulness when writing plugins.

2) Moving the import of `urls` modules to `MyKolibriPlugin.url_module`. This way, we know that loading `kolibri_plugin` is less error prone because it doesn't depend on a functional django stack. So for instance if a plugin has broken something in the Django stack (for instance its views or models are invalid), we can still enable/disable the plugin.

This issue is a bit higher-level and requires design/planning.

@Dm94st , the problem here is that you are connecting to your metadata server instead of the object spawner (requesting a workspace server). In your configuration definition (winiomwin, in this case), you need  iomhost= to point to a host where the object spawner is running (this may be correct already is it's running the same host as metadata server), and your iomport has to be that of the workspace server, not the metadata server; this is wrong for sure, I expect. 
If you have a simple setup, that's all you will need. If you have multiple App Servers (different sets of workspace servers configured, and maybe multiple object spawners), you can use the following info from the doc to see what the hosts and ports are: 
https://sassoftware.github.io/saspy/advanced-topics.html#using-proc-iomoperate-to-find-object-spawner-hosts-and-workspace-server-ports

If you have multiple sets of servers defined, then you'll also need to specify the one you're trying to connect to with the appserver= option (https://sassoftware.github.io/saspy/install.html#remote)

Just using the right port will probably get you running.
Thanks,
Tom

[RFC 5575](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5575) says:

```
7.  Traffic Filtering Actions [...]
Traffic-rate: [...]
         The remaining 4 octets carry the rate information in IEEE
         floating point [IEEE.754.1985] format, units being bytes per
         second.  A traffic-rate of 0 should result on all traffic for
         the particular flow to be discarded.
 ```

Both Cisco and Juniper (I believe) mis-implemented the RFC using bits and not bytes.

Ok.  This was likely my last try at this.

Here's my configuration from front to back:

* ELB using HTTP/S in and HTTP only out to the router(s).  No proxy protocol in play (mutually exclusive with HTTP/S listeners).  HTTPS listener on 443 is configured with a valid wildcard cert.

* Router has `enforceHTTPS` unset.

* Rails application has `config.force_ssl = true`

Still, I cannot trigger a redirect loop.

If I log all the HTTP headers received by the application, these are among them:

```
2016-01-26T16:40:33UTC foobar[web.1]: HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PORT: 443
2016-01-26T16:40:33UTC foobar[web.1]: HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO: https
2016-01-26T16:40:33UTC foobar[web.1]: HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR: 73.68.36.13, 10.21.2.216
2016-01-26T16:40:33UTC foobar[web.1]: HTTP_X_FORWARDED_SSL: on
```

Besides being unable to reproduce the issue, these headers would seem to indicate that the ELB and router are doing precisely the correct thing with respect to SSL offloading.

The only remaining explanation for the redirect loop is that application or application framework that is enforcing the use of HTTPS is not taking the `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_*` headers into account.

Scrolling back through this issue... it's long, contains much speculation and has multiple users reporting multiple problems, reported under varying configurations.  I've tried to investigate each permutation that allegedly triggers a redirect loop and still cannot reproduce.  As we attempt to narrow down the what makes it into the LTS release and what doesn't, it's useful to us to try and close these more nebulous issues.  So... I _am_ going to close this, but I would invite anyone who is still having trouble to open up a new issue that's narrowly scoped to your specific configuration and I would be happy to investigate further.

@kalefranz, @mcg1969 : I need some help on this one. I completely understand why my changes won't work but I need some assistance on generally understanding how to conceptually prioritize features.

Test case T2 above, i.e., the cause of https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6269#issuecomment-344077631, is clearly a bug / something we don't want.

But what about test case T3 (issue #6199)? How should we prioritize features in presence of channel priorities?
Currently, features are something Conda tries to avoid (early minimization of their count), but only for dependencies. That is surprising (and likely unwanted) from a user's perspective, e.g.:
* `conda create -n test -c conda-forge pandas`
  gives `numpy` from `defaults` without feature,
* `conda create -n test -c conda-forge pandas numpy`
  gives `numpy` from `conda-forge` with `blas_openblas` feature.

IMHO, features are penalized too much in that case, but I don't see how to solve this in the current code since I only had a glance at it..

For test case T1 I'm not sure if it's a valid test case. I looks like one can see package builds with different (incl. none) features as completely different packages which just happen to share the same name, version and provide the same functionality (given by the package name). But they don't share the same sequence of build numbers. Hence T1 would not be a valid test case. However (I think) that concept misses the following use case:
We have a software `pkg` with version `ver` and that software does not get updated, hence `pkg` and `ver` are always fixed. Now newer builds of that package all make use of a feature `feat`, e.g., we have the builds:
```
pkg-ver-0
pkg-ver-1
pkg-ver-2   [feat]
pkg-ver-3   [feat]
pkg-ver-4   [feat]
pkg-ver-5   [feat]
...
```
Since `pkg-ver` and `pkg-ver [feat]` are handled as packages with separate/incomparable build sequences and feature counts are minimized early on, the newer builds won't get picked up.


There isn't by any chance a document that describes the solver's behavior formally, right? If not formally, it would also be helpful to have just a (preferable extensive/detailed) list of examples and the corresponding expected behavior.

Yes, that all sounds very reasonable to me, supporting all use cases we determined.

I do not think it is even possible, from the simulator point of view, to mix modules under the same top level requiring different versions of a library. For a simulation there will always be a library.cfg or modelsim.ini that uniquely maps library names to library locations. Thus the scenario you describe means that there are two disjoint set of files where the ones that uses the ISE version cannot depend on files using the Vivado version and vice versa. My recommendation would thus be to not mix modules requiring a different version of an external library in the same run.py but have two run.py files such as run_vivado.py and run_ise.py. You can still share code between two run.py files since it is just Python.
Would that work for you?

To support this in VUnit we would have to add multiple compile contexts where one file from one compile context cannot depend on a file in another compile context but maybe on files in a parent compile context. This is similar to having multiple run.py files but the testcases could run in the same suite and there could be common dependencies between the sets of files. This is not a quick job and probably not something that we should do if this is not a very common problem. I think people will use either Vivado or ISE in their project not both.

Waiting for VHDL-2017 to be implemented by your vendor is another approach. History tells us that can be a long wait.

There are of course other options available. Write a standalone code generator and keep the generated code under version control. Write a lint tool that fails your test suite when there are missing checks. If we just had a suitable open source VHDL parser for the task...

By default Elasticsearch is available on port 9200. Which is why `elasticsearch-py` behaves this way. If you want to use a different port you can specify any port you want. 

It looks like you are using Elasticsearch running on port 80. (unless you're using https, then it would be port 443). 

Default ports: 
* http://blah -> port 80
* https://blah -> port 443

I would recommend not altering the elasticsearch-py code and instead instantiate your connection using the port that your elasticsearch instance is running on. 


```python
es = Elasticsearch(
    hosts=[{'host': host, 'port': 443}],
    http_auth=awsauth,
    use_ssl=True,
    verify_certs=True,
    connection_class=RequestsHttpConnection
)
```

The CLI/RBAC uses AAD-Graph instead of MS-Graph. You are right, the endpoint is `graph.windows.net` 
For `set the owner of e.g. a Resource group` you don't go through the graph permission, rather go through role assignment, e.g. `az role assignment create -g myResourceGroup --role owner`
The error of `Forbidden for url: https://graph.windows.net/` is caused by you logged in using a service principal, which hasn't been given the permission to AAD graph and you need to configure separately. So check out [this comment](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-node/issues/2363#issuecomment-354897064). Note 'az ad app create' would also let you configure the permission, but you still need to go to portal and click the button of `Grant Permission` to settle all, so you are better off just doing all in the portal
It is definitely not a trivial process, but due to the limitation at the service level, CLI is not able to configure all for you.
I am closing this since this is not a CLI bug, but feel free to post any follow-up questions here. And sorry for the delay in response.

Is https://travis-ci.org/bulletphysics/bullet3/jobs/279592908 with this patch or no? 

I think I'm seeing a chicken and egg problem here:
1. if it just finds some python version, and you're running 3.5, you could get 2.7 and 3.5 wouldn't work. 
2. If you don't make sure all the python versions are consistent, you could get 2.7 for the libs and 3.5 for the interpreter and then the install/execution breaks. 
3. When `n` python versions are installed with a correct auto-detect implementation, not specifying the python version will fail `n-1/n` of the time, but specifying the python version you want in advance will work 100% of the time.

Auto-detect across python 2 and 3 previously appeared to work in some cases but contained a bug, namely item 2 above. 

This patch resolves that by specifying the version in advance and choosing a reasonable default. Reverting this patch leaves it in the broken state 2. As I understand things, this PR works correctly to resolve that problem 2, as designed.

Is that description all consistent with your understanding of things? What would you find satisfactory?

There are two different answers.

For Google's, it's not present because it's not a root CA: it's an intermediary CA. The list is primarily intended to contain only trust roots. The issuer of the Google Internet Authority G2 certificate *is* present in the trust bundle ("GeoTrust Global CA"), so as long as the site in question is sending their intermediate CA certificate (which they should be), it'll be trusted.

As to Equifax, the certificate in question is a 1024-bit root certificate. These are formally deprecated because they are weak and vulnerable to attack. For this reason, they are not in the trust store. However, because users frequently still need them, you can find it in `certifi.old_where()`. This is also related to #26.

This is a bit complicated because setuptools can depend on certifi, and a system that wishes to express that dependency chain will end up with a circular dependency. A similar problem exists for the packaging library and we use the setuptools/distutils fallback to achieve this, see [pypa/package:setup.py](https://github.com/pypa/packaging/blob/c22fbd8c7bfa989df911243b10901645b7d4758d/setup.py#L10-L19).

If certifi cares about this use case, I suggest just stealing that block.

Okay, here's a possible sequence. I haven't tested this.

1. The notebook launches
2. The user quickly presses shift-enter to execute a cell that has widget code.
3. The cell is queued up for execution
4. The widget javascript is loaded. When the widget javascript is loaded, it immediately sends a comm message to the kernel with the version information, etc. This is comm message is queued *behind* the cell execution. (TODO: Check that the comm open message to jupyter.widget.version is queued behind the cells)
5. The kernel starts up.
6. The cell is executed. Since the kernel widgets has not seen the comm message from the javascript, it assumes there is no widget javascript and so prints an error message: "Widget Javascript not detected.  It may not be installed or enabled properly."

Basically, it's a race condition between the widget javascript running before the user executes a cell, and an assumption from the kernel side that the ipywidgets will be able to execute a comm open before any user code runs.

Before #1002, here's what I am thinking was happening in the situations where the message seemed to randomly pop up.

1. The notebook launches
2. The user quickly presses shift-enter to execute a cell that has widget code.
3. The execution is silently ignored since the kernel is not up yet.
4. The widget javascript is loaded. When the widget javascript is loaded, it immediately sends a comm message to the kernel with the version information, etc. *This is also silently ignored!*
5. The kernel starts up.
6. Another cell is executed. Since the kernel widgets has not seen the comm message from the javascript, it assumes there is no widget javascript and so prints an error message: "Widget Javascript not detected.  It may not be installed or enabled properly."

It always seemed fickle when the 'js not installed' error came up.

I think the real problem is that in ipywidgets, we are assuming that the widget javascript will have priority access to a kernel, before user execution. I think we probably need a more robust method for doing the version check.

And clearly this ipywidget check is faulty if we have multiple clients talking to the same kernel. The first client will okay its version of javascript, so the error won't show if the second client does not have the javascript installed.

I've thought about this a bit and I don't think that the description should be part of annotations (#333). Annotations should be for *arbitrary* and *domain-specific* metadata, like the `as_sql_col` example, whereas, the description of a field is fairly universal.  If we choose to relegate this to "annotations", then we miss the opportunity to define a standard:  some users will choose "description", while others choose "help" or "doc".  This will undermine the stated goal of allowing schematics to serve as a universal model for converting to other standards, like jsonschema.

Hi Mike,

that's currently not possible.

librdkafka will create one internal main thread and one thread per broker.
If a bootstrap broker exactly matches (by name and port) an advertised broker it will be promoted to a real broker and the thread will be re-used, otherwise a new broker thread will be created.

So, best case: 1+N threads (N=brokers in cluster), worst case: 1+(N*2) if all brokers in cluster are specified as bootstrap but dont match (e.g., missing domain part, or using ip address or some alias).
(The port defaults to 9092 if not specified).

The per-broker thread design was meant to provide parallelism on modern hardware and is highly effective if partitions are evenly balanced across brokers.

Are you seeing a problem with the thread count?

Is a period equivalent to a partial datetime? I thought periods were supposed to be something with a discrete start and end. `partialdatetime` is more of a generalization of the concept of datetime naivete that you see partially, so for example:

1. `date` is equivalent to `partialdatetime(y, m, d, None, None, None, None)`
2. `time` is equivalent to `partialdatetime(None, None, None, h, m, s, us)`
3. naive datetimes are equivalent to `partialdatetime(y, m, d, h, m, s, us, None)`.

Period of time is I think closer to either `(datetime, timedelta)` (or `(datetime, relativedelta)`) or `(datetime, datetime)`.

@alan-unravel I don't think there's a real conflict here. For ambiguous dates like `010203`, there are 6 different possible interpretations:

1. YYMMDD
2. YYDDMM
3. MMDDYY
4. DDMMYY
5. MMYYDD
6. DDYYMM

5 and 6, with year in the middle, is *not* a standard way of writing dates and so dateutil doesn't make that guess, so we're left with 4 possible options and 2 flags to specify them.  Per #81, the mapping goes:

```
yearfirst | dayfirst | Format
------------------------------
   False  |   False  |  MMDDYY 
   False  |   True   |  DDMMYY
   True   |   False  |  YYMMDD
   True   |   True   |  YYDDMM
```

As you can see, the operation of `yearfirst` is to move the interpretation of "year" in front of the Month/Day combination, and the operation of `dayfirst` is to move the interpretation of "day" in front of "month".

Actually, YAML considers duplicate keys an error. From http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html:

> JSON's RFC4627 requires that mappings keys merely “SHOULD” be unique, while YAML insists they “MUST” be. Technically, YAML therefore complies with the JSON spec, choosing to treat duplicates as an error. In practice, since JSON is silent on the semantics of such duplicates, the only portable JSON files are those with unique keys, which are therefore valid YAML files.

and

> Since YAML mappings require key uniqueness, representations must include a mechanism for testing the equality of nodes. This is non-trivial since YAML allows various ways to format scalar content. For example, the integer eleven can be written as “0o13” (octal) or “0xB” (hexadecimal). If both notations are used as keys in the same mapping, only a YAML processor which recognizes integer formats would correctly flag the duplicate key as an error.

Perhaps this should be considered a bug in ruamel.yaml? It should throw an exception, rather than silently ignoring all but the last entry.

I agree with you. It doesn't hurt to set `src_last_modified_millis` for every file upload. Checking the difference between local and server time also seems useful, but this is a separate issue that should also apply to sync.

Using last N seconds instead of total average is one way. Another way would be to make a solution which is kind of a bridge between what we have now and what you suggest, to get an accurate estimation:

Lets say there are two speeds: average speed and current speed.
If the user is currently occupying his network to do something short (send a 10MB email or download an ISO image, for example), then there is a good chance that the current speed will return back to the average speed soon. The chance of such thing happening decreases with the increasing time in which the current speed is being held at the same level.

We use the `tqdm` library to render the progress bars and the speed reporting, as well as ETA reporting for uploads and downloads (currently there is no ETA for sync). I think the changes to the mechanism in question should be implemented in the `tqdm` project, not in b2.

Would you be willing to take a shot at implementing the method of reporting speed, as the one you have described? We'd provide support at the B2 CLI side to integrate it when the option becomes available.

I wasn't sure how to do an accurate estimate for sync.  It tracks both number of files and number of bytes.  Using bytes is good for big files, but if you have a lot of small files it would not be very accurate.

If you sync one big file first, and then a lot of small files, basing the estimate on bytes will say you're 99% done after the big file, even though each small file takes some time, and only 50% of the elapsed time is done.

I think I see your point now. Patching `ordered` after the options class is instantiated means that a custom `SchemaOpts` wouldn't be able to force the value of `ordered`.

```python
from marshmallow import SchemaOpts, Schema

class CustomOpts(SchemaOpts):

    def __init__(self, meta):
        super().__init__(meta)
        self.ordered = False


class MySchema(Schema):
    OPTIONS_CLASS = CustomOpts

    class Meta:
        ordered = True

assert MySchema().opts.ordered is False
```

I just didn't like having a special-cased option in the constructor, but I think it's fine as long as we have a documented migration path.

> Perhaps some pre_load edge cases might suffer from the change. Any real-life example, anyone?

I've got production code that reloes on the fact that a nested field with `many=True` and `@pre_dump(pass_many=True)` on its schema will execute this `pre_dump` **only once**.

The benefit is making sure expensive code that is needed to be executed before nested fields can be dumped is not repeated for each element in the `fields.List`.

I don't really see this as an edge case though, pretty sure doing something to the whole set of related objects before dumping/loading them is a common pattern.

@dacoex good/bad is subjective, but I'd say that the state of solar power forecasting is bad. Of course, people trying to sell forecasts say that their model is the best and people trying to get grant funding say that their model is great but would be so much better if you'd just give them a little bit of money, please, please, please. The problem is that weather is so complicated and varies so much across the world that it becomes very difficult to make objective comparisons without quickly getting lost in the details. This is perhaps even more true for clouds and thus irradiance. Finally, if you add a weather-to-irradiance-to-power black box to your analysis then it becomes harder still to tease out the real differences between forecasts.

My proposal is that the new code can make the objective comparisons a little more rigorous and a little bit easier. First, it makes it trivial to access the data related to PV generation from many U.S. weather models. Then you can use the preexisting pvlib functions on that data, or you can do whatever you want with it. If you choose the latter, hopefully you'll now be in an open source mindset and publish (in some form) your analysis code in addition to the results. Either way, it establishes a traceable, open benchmark tool for forecast comparisons. 

So, like the rest of pvlib, what I'm proposing is a tool. I'm not making any claims as to the accuracy of the weather models, or the Liu Jordan model, or the SAPM, or the combinations of them. The tool certainly does not solve all problems related to objective, reproducible research in the solar power forecasting field, but I think/hope that people will find it useful.

Quick notes regarding a few other points above... 

1. All weather models are a mix of approximated physics, plus a lot of parameterizations, so, they're both empirical and physical. pvlib adds another layer of empiricism: the Liu Jordan cloud-cover-to-irradiance model. This is, to me, the least interesting part of the proposal. We just picked an old, standard reference model to get started, but there's an infinite number of ways to improve this.
2. The interesting MERRA discussion perhaps suggests that much of the "forecasting" code actually belongs in a new io module (see #29), with the forecast data being just another data source along with TMY and other standard datasets. Much of the motivation for the forecasts is similar to the motivation for standard datasets such as TMY, which further suggests that they could be grouped together. I'll have to think about this more.
2. All of this is helpful feedback for how to develop the PVSC abstract into a talk or poster and a paper. I'll be the first to admit that we need to add a lot more references to the literature in the final paper. And yes, @jforbess, that is an embarrassing mix up, thank you for pointing it out.

On to the dependencies and packaging... The main new optional dependency, Siphon, is a bit esoteric at this point, but Unidata has made developing Python tools a priority for the foreseeable future, so I'm not too worried about it. Installing Siphon itself is not a big deal, but it depends on NetCDF, which requires slightly more experience/luck to compile (though not nearly as bad as scipy). The anaconda packages for NetCDF have been reliable, though. The forecast module is not imported in the ``__init__.py`` file, so if you don't install the optional dependencies then you can continue to use pvlib as if it never existed. These are not arguments for including the code in pvlib, only that it's not making it any harder to install pvlib.

Thanks again for your feedback.

The commit with hash `5fe129b` in mtrberzi/z3, on the `develop` branch, definitely has this bug. I've put this in its own branch, `implication-bug` in the same repository, for quick access.

I've pushed, in the latest commit to `implication-bug`, a file `test.smt` that contains a test case revealing this bug. Model generation is not working in this branch, so an assertion will fail after final check, but compile with tracing enabled and run with `-tr:t_str -tr:t_str_detail` to see the strange output I saw.

In detail, in `final_check_eh` I dump all assignments, and notice the solver has entered a state where it thinks all of the following are true:

```
-------- [t_str_detail] final_check_eh ../src/smt/theory_str.cpp:632 ---------
dumping all assignments:
(= (Concat X Y) "")
(=> (= (Concat X Y) "") (= (Length "") (Length (Concat X Y))))
(=> (= (Concat X Y) "") (and (= X "") (= Y "")))
(>= (Length (Concat X Y)) 0)
(= (= (Concat X Y) "") (= (Length (Concat X Y)) 0))
(>= (Length X) 0)
(= (= X "") (= (Length X) 0))
(>= (Length Y) 0)
(= (= Y "") (= (Length Y) 0))
(= (Length "") 0)
(= (Length (Concat X Y)) 0)
(<= (Length (Concat X Y)) 0)
(= (Length "") (Length (Concat X Y)))
(not (= (Length X) 0))
(not (<= (Length X) 0))
(not (= X ""))
(not (and (= X "") (= Y "")))
(not (= Y ""))
(not (= (Length Y) 0))
(not (<= (Length Y) 0))
------------------------------------------------
```

Of note, the following excerpt:
```
(= (Concat X Y) "")
(=> (= (Concat X Y) "") (and (= X "") (= Y "")))
(not (= X ""))
(not (and (= X "") (= Y "")))
(not (= Y ""))
```

The assertion `(=> (= (Concat X Y) "") (and (= X "") (= Y "")))` is an axiom, which is added in `smt/theory_str.cpp:382`.

As a temporary solution this will work but we would appreciate it if you could rewrite the recursive could so that the stack-overflow doesn't occur anymore.

The [default tactic](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.cpp) (which is what you get via `(check-sat)`) first runs the simplifier (`simplify` tactic) and then tries to figure out which logic your benchmarks and then runs a tactic for that logic. If it doesn't find anything suitable, it will run the `smt` tactic. 

That means `(check-sat)` should (I think) in your case be the same as `(check-sat-using (then simplify smt))`, but it isn't. This is due to the `(push 1)` command, which is handled differently when using `check-sat-using` and possibly also due to bugs causing some parameters not passed on correctly through the tactic framework (we fixed a bunch, but I'm sure some are still in there).

> If we decide not to worry about it, that would mean we are free to rename `Layer`, right?

If we decide not to worry about `isinstance` checks, we can use a different name for the layer base class (i.e., the one subclassing `object`). Good question! What you want here is:

`stats = match.participants[summoner].stats`

The way to think about it is like this:  `p = match.participants[summoner]` gives me the participant in the match that corresponds to the summoner I want. Then I want the stats of that participant, so I run `stats = p.stats`. Those two lines of code can be combined into the one I wrote above: `stats = match.participants[summoner].stats`.

So first you get the participant that you care about, then you get that participant's stats.

@teto 
``cmd2`` has the "normal" ``cmd2`` way of doing commands, but it also has the much, much nicer way using the ``@options`` decorator.  The ``@options`` decorator uses **optparse** to parse options, but it also automatically uses shell.split() on the args.  So an ``@options`` command definition looks like the following

```Python
@options([make_option('-p', '--piglatin', action="store_true", help="atinLay"),
              make_option('-s', '--shout', action="store_true", help="N00B EMULATION MODE"),
              make_option('-r', '--repeat', type="int", help="output [n] times")], arg_desc='positional_arg_string')
    def do_oprint(self, arg, opts=None):
        """Print the options and argument list this options command was called with."""
        print('oprint was called with the following\n\toptions: {!r}\n\targuments: {!r}'.format(opts, arg))
```

Note that these command accept **two** arguments instead of just the default one:  ``arg`` and ``opts``.  In this case ``arg`` is a list of argument strings which has already been pre-parsed with shlex but in an intelligent manner where unecessary extra quotes have been stripped.

This is so convenient, that I use the ``@options`` decorator for **ALL** commands, sometimes with an empty list of options.

So the whole point of this issue is to figure out an intelligent way to have "normal" commands get parsed in a similar fashion to the ``@options`` commands in terms of that argument splitting.

You've installed ``python-ldap`` instead of ``pyldap``.

``django-ldapdb`` relies on the ``pyldap`` fork of ``python-ldap``, whose goal is mainly to add Python3 support — which you'll require anyway starting from Django 2.0 (which should be released in 8 months).

You should be able to fix this by running:
```sh
pip uninstall python-ldap
pip uninstall pyldap
pip install pyldap
```

Interesting use-case, however, if someone writes an installer which does something like you describe, it is very likely the plugin also needs to copy/deploy the certificate at some remote location as well, (unless the certbot keystore is on a shared filesystem; this is why I opened ticket #4046 earlier). 
In that case, the author of the plugin can decide to integrate the restart command as part of deploy_certificate() (or renew_deploy_certificate()) method and solve the restart after every deploy within the plugin itself without relying on certbot to call restart() every time.
However the other way around it is not possible. At this point it is not possible to limit the amount of restart() calls executed by the renew handler. Of course it is solvable with post-hook commands, and leaving restart() empty in the plugin, but in my opinion that a bit hacky.
An other option would be to introduce a reload() call, which can be called every time, and a restart() which is called only once per installer type in the renewal case.

The errors are appearing in the log file because your code configures the root logger to send all messages to the `nohup.out` log file. The parse error is being logged by [Shapely's GEOS module](https://github.com/Toblerity/Shapely/blob/f9111c833a511f243235f4b9128d20f10dd7ec73/shapely/geos.py#L223-L227), using the name `shapely.geos`, but will be handled by the root logger unless it has been overridden with something more specific. We can reconfigure it to only print errors to the terminal like this:

```python
import sys
...
log = logging.getLogger('shapely.geos')
ch = logging.StreamHandler(stream=sys.stderr)
ch.setLevel(logging.ERROR)
log.addHandler(ch)
log.propagate = False
```

Hope that helps!

An unexpected consequence of this feature is that the options now have to be handled for local host because they might trigger file copy/rename. There is also a problem with `rsync` in that the files will be first copied normally and renamed, so it might overwrite a file that should not be overwritten.

This idea is difficult to implement because it is difficult to catch what the subkernels send. For example, the R kernel sends `display_data`, not `execution_result`, so there is no way to learn the type of output. It does send `text/markdown` alone with `text/plain` but the `markdown` seems to be wrapped.

If we have to rely on the sos code cell to generate report, the `%preview` magic could be enough and all we need might be a shortcut to hide/display input cells.
Option `name` is now deprecated because it mostly overlaps with the new `tags` parameter.

But in general I agree that developing a **general** solution for variable exchange among languages can be an appealing feature and can expand the user-base of sos greatly. I mean, people can use `sos_import` and `sos_export` in their scripts without using sos, even if these functions are simple wrappers of native functions.

On the other hand, whereas in-memory data exchange in sos is transient and can be changed/improved freely without affecting user interface, these on-disk files are more likely to be used in a less transient manner, which will require much more complex implementation in general. Whereas this feature is well within the scope of "SoS", it would have to be planned and implemented very carefully, with perhaps support from a major grant.

@jph00 I have some comments/questions about the sphinx implementation: 

Sphinx takes text files written in [restructuredText (rst)](https://www.southampton.ac.uk/~fangohr/computing/rst/rst.txt) and transforms them into html (although it can generate other outputs as well, like pdfs). In `fastai/docs` there are files written in markdown and asiidoc. Sphinx can work with docs that are written in both markdown and rst, but because markdown and rst are so similar, and because sphinx was built to work with rst, I think that it would probably be simpler to just rewrite the existing docs in rst.

There are other documentation generators, and some that use markdown as their basis, but I think that sphinx seems pretty powerful and useful. I don't think it would be too much trouble to rewrite the existing docs - there aren't too many yet. Sphinx is also easy to integrate with [readTheDocs](https://docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) if that's something fastai is interested in as well

If we do want to use sphinx and re-write the docs into rst, that's probably the first thing I'd do

Thanks, I think the error is essentially the following. The timeline combines information from two sources: the "task table" which contains a record of all tasks and their dependencies, and the "event log" which contains profiling information. Whenever a job finishes, we flush all of the tasks for that job out of the task table, but not from the event log (@concretevitamin does that sound right?), resulting in a mismatch. Then in the visualization, we loop over the event log and look for all of the corresponding tasks in the task table, but some are not present.

That suggests that you should only see this problem when running multiple jobs on the same cluster, is that consistent with what you're seeing?

It makes sense to list any application-bindable inputs/outputs of the graph in the onnx::GraphProto, but it's dubious to list the names of tensors for _all_ internal nodes that just take static initializer inputs for weights. For example, the squeezenet ONNX file lists every single node's input tensor in the onnx::GraphProto input, but the graph really only has one bindable tensor of interest to an application (data_0), which gets lost in the noise.

Hello,

Thanks for the insights. I tried understanding what is going on inside the reconstructor, with modest success, so instead I started making a list of things that work and things that don't. My hope would be to understand which are due to errors in the grammar (i.e., a badly written grammar for code reconstruction purposes) and which are due to errors in Lark (that could be fixed).

Most of these are minimalist examples from errors I encountered while trying to reconstruct with the .lark grammar. 
All use LALR. and the following terminals:

```
_NL: /(\\r?\\n)+\s*/
%import common (WS_INLINE, NUMBER, WORD)
%ignore WS_INLINE
```

Example 1: Starred object
--------------------------
*Grammar*
```
start: (rule | _NL)*
rule: WORD ":" WORD
```

*Code to be parsed and reconstructed*
```
SALUT: BONJOUR
```

*Error*
`lark.exceptions.ParseError: Infinite recursion detected! (rule <NonTerminal('__anon_star_0') : NonTerminal(Token(RULE, '__anon_star_0'))>)`

*Comments*
Same thing happens with a plus instead of a star. Does not seem to cause a problem when included deeper inside a rule, as in the json parser. This seems like an error in Lark?


Example 2: Starred rule (works!)
--------------------------------
*Grammar*
```
start: item*
item: NL
    | rule
rule: WORD ":" NUMBER
```

*Code to be parsed and reconstructed*
```
ABC: 1
DEF: 2
```

*Comments*
Example 1 works when rephrased as in this example so that no parenthesised object is starred.
However, if `NL` is written as an ignored terminal `_NL`, then the parsed tree looks like this:
`Tree(start, [Tree(item, []), Tree(item, [Tree(rule, [Token(WORD, 'ABC'), Token(NUMBER, '1')])]), ...`
...and the reconstructor fails with
`AttributeError: 'WriteTokensTransformer' object has no attribute 'item'`
...because the first new line in the code cannot be reconstructed. This seems like a grammar specification error to me?

Example 3: Extra output in reconstructed code
-----------------------------------------------
*Grammar*
```
start: line*
?line: NL
    | rule
    | "hello"
```

*Code to be parsed and reconstructed*
```
ABC: 1
DEF: 2
```

*Error*
Printing the reconstructed text gives, oddly enough, an extra "hello" at the beginning!
```
hello
ABC:1
DEF:2
```

*Comments*
Works fine if `line` is not preceded by `?`.

Example 4: Alias
----------------
*Grammar*
```
start: line*
line: NL
    | rule
    | "hello" -> hi
```

*Code to be parsed and reconstructed*
```
ABC: 1
hello
```

*Error*
`AttributeError: 'Tree' object has no attribute 'pos_in_stream'`

*Comments*
Works fine with no alias. If, however, `hello` is placed before `| rule`, then we get ```AttributeError: 'WriteTokensTransformer' object has no attribute 'line'``` followed by ```KeyError: 'HELLO'```


Sorry for the very long post! Hope this can help improve the reconstructor and also help me find my way around the code. By the way, running the json parser example, I now get ```lark.exceptions.GrammarError: Using an undefined rule: NonTerminal(Token(RULE, '__anon_star_0'))```. Any idea why that happened?

Regards,
Julien
